decision: Use SEC bulk Form N-PORT Data Sets (quarterly ZIPs of tab-delimited files) as the primary data source.
rationale: Verified against SEC primary sources: pre-parsed, joinable flat files covering fund identity, monthly returns, and holdings. Avoids scraping individual XML filings entirely.

decision: Identify the US equity fund universe using Yahoo Finance categories as a pre-filter, cross-checked against holdings-based inference (value-weighted ASSET_CAT='EC' + INVESTMENT_COUNTRY='US').
rationale: N-PORT has no fund-level equity/strategy label, so it must be inferred from holdings. Yahoo categories (joined via SEC's official mutual-fund ticker map) give a fast, independent cross-check, but are treated as a heuristic since yfinance is an unofficial scraper.

decision: Build peer groups ourselves via cosine similarity + clustering over holdings-based strategy vectors, then validate the clusters against Yahoo fund categories (purity / adjusted Rand index).
rationale: Yahoo categories could have been used directly as peer groups, but that would skip building real holdings-based similarity/clustering. Using them as a validator instead keeps the clustering work while still getting an external sanity check.

decision: Define 'underperform' as a fund's next-quarter total return falling below its cluster's median return, evaluated across multiple pooled quarterly prediction periods (not a single train/test split).
rationale: N-PORT holdings snapshots are effectively quarterly (only the 3rd month of each quarter is historically public), but each filing reports all 3 monthly returns — this maps cleanly onto a quarter-over-quarter prediction setup with several periods pooled for a realistic time-based split.

decision: Use phi-4 (Q4_K_S) served locally by LM Studio for plain-English narration of results only (RAG-style, grounded in computed data) — not for training or fine-tuning.
rationale: The quantized GGUF model cannot be trained, and the core data (fund holdings/returns) is numeric — a poor fit for LLM prediction. The LLM's strength here is explaining results already produced by classical ML, not producing the predictions itself.

decision: Run the project as a step-wise-methodology build: a contract-first plan.md, one gated milestone at a time, each with its own design.md and a human approval gate before the next step starts.
rationale: Keeps each stage reviewable and reversible, and avoids docs drifting from code since decisions/workflow docs update in the same change as each step.

decision: Set the fund universe target to 1000 resolved funds, using an iterative-fill sampling strategy (shuffle the full complete-panel candidate pool, resolve ticker+Yahoo data one at a time until 1000 succeed) rather than a fixed pre-filter sample.
rationale: A 20-fund pilot run showed ~45% of candidate series have no ticker mapping at all before Yahoo is even attempted; a fixed sample of exactly max_funds candidates would under-shoot the target after attrition. The complete 12-quarter panel has 9,445 candidate series, giving ample headroom.

decision: Corrected the US-equity flag to require BOTH yahoo_stock_position >= 0.80 (asset class) AND a holdings-based US-domiciled-equity share >= 0.70 (geography), instead of the asset-class signal alone.
rationale: Empirically confirmed yfinance's stockPosition only measures stock-vs-bond allocation, not geography - international and emerging-market equity funds also show stockPosition near 1.0. Combining it with a value-weighted holdings check (ASSET_CAT='EC' + INVESTMENT_COUNTRY='US') fixed 87/90 misflagged international funds in testing; the 3 remaining exceptions trace to a documented, accepted N-PORT data limitation (fund-of-funds wrappers reporting their issuer's country, not their economic exposure).

decision: Rejected category-only feature vectors (asset_cat/issuer_type/country) for fund clustering, in favor of building strategy vectors from actual portfolio overlap - since the step2 universe is already equity-filtered, category fields alone are nearly identical across all 363 funds and carry almost no discriminative signal.
rationale: Caught before implementation: clustering on near-identical category vectors would produce a degenerate result (one dominant cluster), defeating the purpose of peer grouping.

decision: Built and empirically tested issuer-overlap vectors (value-weighted, then breadth-weighted top-K held issuers) for clustering, then rejected them in favor of local text-embeddings of a holdings description (sentence-transformers, all-MiniLM-L6-v2).
rationale: Issuer-overlap vectors scored near chance-level agreement with Yahoo fund categories (adjusted Rand Index 0.01-0.07, confirmed insensitive to K and to cluster count 5-60 - ruled out as a tuning problem). Embeddings of a text description of each fund's top holdings raised ARI to ~0.25, roughly a 10x improvement, while running fully locally and fast (~60s for the full 12-quarter pipeline). Root cause of the overlap approach's failure: popular mega-cap stocks are held broadly across growth/value/blend categories, so raw stock overlap mostly captures 'which companies are popular' rather than value/growth style, which text embeddings apparently capture better.

decision: Added a GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/tests.yml) running the fast pytest suite on every push/PR to main. Confirmed no CI/CD existed previously (no .github/workflows directory, and 3 commits sat unpushed on origin before this).
rationale: The 10 unit/smoke tests are fast (~5s) and need no network access or secrets (verified by running them with the data/ directory temporarily removed, simulating a clean checkout), making them well-suited to CI. The full pipeline (step1 ingestion: ~15+ min, GBs of downloads, needs SEC_USER_AGENT and live external APIs) deliberately stays out of CI and is only run manually per gated step, per the step-wise-methodology approach used throughout this project.

decision: Fixed a real bug in compute_quarterly_returns: np.prod() on a pandas Series silently skips NaN via Series.prod(skipna=True), so a fund with one unparseable monthly return (2 funds affected, from step1's pd.to_numeric coercion) got a silently-wrong 2-of-3-month product for that quarter instead of a correctly-NaN result.
rationale: Caught by cross-checking .describe() row counts between fund_metrics_overall (correctly NaN via .values, a numpy array) and fund_metrics_quarterly (incorrectly non-NaN) for the same 2 funds - the discrepancy revealed the inconsistency. Fixed by using .values in the quarterly calc too, with a regression test added.

decision: Migrated the persistence layer from standalone .parquet files to a single embedded SQL database (DuckDB, data/processed/fundspeers.duckdb), on request. PostgreSQL was considered and rejected in favor of DuckDB.
rationale: PostgreSQL needs a running server process and connection credentials - real new infrastructure this project doesn't otherwise have. DuckDB is serverless (a single file, no credentials) and gives the same real-SQL experience (joins, aggregates, window functions, any DuckDB client). Migrated existing parquet output directly into the new file (no recompute needed) and confirmed every step's code needed zero changes, validating the fundspeers.io abstraction built in step0.

decision: Built step4's features as trailing (point-in-time-safe) metrics computed fresh from monthly_returns, rather than reusing step3's fund_metrics_overall - and used step2's category-tier bucketing (not raw cluster_id) as the only cross-quarter-comparable categorical feature.
rationale: fund_metrics_overall is computed from a fund's ENTIRE 36-month history, so using it as a feature 'at quarter Q' would leak information from quarters after Q. Separately, step2's cluster_id is an arbitrary per-quarter KMeans label with no meaning across quarters, so pooling it as a feature across the 11 quarterly transitions would be meaningless; yahoo_category (bucketed into Large/Mid/Small/Sector tiers) is stable across quarters and used instead. Both risks were designed around before writing code, then verified with dedicated unit tests (point-in-time-safety, split leakage) rather than discovered afterward.

decision: Random forest (AUC 0.710) beats both the coin-flip baseline (0.500) and a single decision tree (0.638) on next-quarter peer-underperformance, using a time-based split (train on 2022-2023, test on 2024) - confirmed on real data, not just the Required Output's minimum bar.
rationale: Feature importances are dominated by the fund's own recent performance (return_vs_cluster_median_q, trailing drawdown/Sharpe/return) rather than fund size or category tier, which is a plausible pattern (not a sign of a spurious or leaking feature).

decision: step5_narrate generates narrations on demand (via narrate_fund()) rather than precomputing a persisted table for all 4,356 fund-quarters; the conductor's run() only narrates one illustrative example (the highest predicted-underperformance-probability fund in the test split).
rationale: There's no requirement to narrate the whole panel, and doing so would be slow/costly for a local model with no clear use for the bulk output. Verified end-to-end against the live LM Studio server (confirmed running phi-4 at http://127.0.0.1:1234/v1): every claim in a real narration (ticker, category, all four whole-history metrics, quarterly return, cluster id, purity/ARI, two named peers, predicted probability, actual outcome) was spot-checked against the raw retrieved context and matched exactly - no invented numbers or peers. Both documented edge cases (no prediction available, no resolved cluster) were tested directly and handled gracefully.

decision: Added hard (deterministic, rule-based) cluster definitions - dominant category/tier and average risk metrics per (quarter, cluster_id) - rather than an LLM-generated description, keeping clusters' numeric ids as their only label until now.
rationale: Clusters previously had no human-readable identity at all, just an arbitrary per-quarter number. A rule-based definition (majority category, average Sharpe/volatility) keeps the same LLM boundary already established in step5 - the model narrates already-computed facts, it never labels or decides anything itself. Lives in step3 (needs fund_metrics_overall, which doesn't exist until step3 runs) even though it describes step2's clusters. Needing category_tier a third time here (after step2 and step4) triggered extracting it into a shared fundspeers/category.py module instead of copy-pasting again - verified as a pure refactor by re-running step2 and confirming byte-identical purity/ARI results.

decision: Added step6_out_of_sample: sampled 1000 new funds fully disjoint from the original training set, ran the same pipeline (ingest/similarity/metrics) against a parallel '_oos' table namespace, and evaluated the step4 model two ways - frozen (never refit) and retrained on the combined original+OOS set.
rationale: Both were requested and both answer different questions. Frozen-model AUC on 1000 new never-trained-on funds was 0.693 vs. the original 0.710 test AUC - only a modest drop, real evidence the model generalizes to new funds rather than memorizing quirks of the original 363. Retrained-on-combined AUC was 0.725, a further modest improvement consistent with more diverse data helping a little. ingest.py/similarity.py/metrics.py's run() functions gained backward-compatible exclude_series/table_suffix parameters (defaults preserve original behavior exactly, verified via a small-scope disjointness test before the full 1000-fund run) rather than duplicating the pipeline. step4 now persists the fitted model (joblib) bundled with its exact training feature-column list, since nothing had saved the model object itself before - only its predictions.

decision: Added cluster_definitions.short_title, a compact allocation-only bucket name (e.g. "Leaning Large Blend", "Concentrated Energy Limited Partnership") - built from dominant_category_share and dominant_category only, deliberately excluding any performance stat (avg_sharpe, avg_volatility).
rationale: User caught two real problems with an earlier draft that included a performance word: (1) clusters are formed from holdings/allocation similarity, not performance, so baking a performance stat into the identifying name implies the cluster was formed because of its performance, which isn't true; (2) a performance band worded as "Underperforming" collides with this pipeline's existing, different meaning of underperform (relative to a fund's own cluster median, from step4) - a cluster can't underperform itself. The longer title field still reports avg_volatility/avg_sharpe as descriptive stats, just not folded into the cluster's identity.

decision: Extracted concentration_word/compute_dominant_category_info into shared fundspeers/category.py (used by both step2's cluster-map legend and step3's cluster_definitions table), and replaced the cluster map's numeric cluster_id colorbar with a proper legend showing each cluster's short_title.
rationale: short_title only needs dominant_category/dominant_category_share, not the metrics-dependent fields step3 also computes, so it can be (and now is) computed directly in step2 before step3 ever runs - avoiding a third near-duplicate of the same logic. Comparing the original and OOS cluster maps with real names visible (not just colors) made a genuine structural consistency check possible: the separated left-island cluster in both plots is dominated by Target-Date allocation funds, even though the arbitrary cluster_id differs between the two independent KMeans fits.

decision: Confirmed the original-vs-OOS cluster label mismatch (different colors/ids for the same visual island) is expected, not a bug: both similarity.py's KMeans and PCA are freshly re-fit on each run's own data, never reusing the other run's fitted centroids/transform - and the two runs use fully disjoint funds anyway, so ARI/AMI between them isn't even computable.
rationale: User asked directly whether OOS was re-clustered independently or scored against the existing fitted model; verified from the code (KMeans(...).fit_predict(...) inside the per-quarter loop, called fresh every run()) rather than assumed. The right structural check is each run's own purity/ARI against Yahoo categories (already computed), not cross-run label agreement.

decision: Promoted the retrained-on-combined model (754 funds, test AUC=0.725) to be the new official models/random_forest_model.joblib, replacing the original 363-fund model - backed up to random_forest_model_original_363funds.joblib rather than silently overwritten, and regenerated fund_predictions/model_feature_importances so the persisted tables stay consistent with whichever model is actually saved as official.
rationale: User explicitly opted in after being told the retrained model was never persisted before (only its AUC was reported) - promoting is a deliberate, explicit action, not an automatic side effect of running step6's evaluation.

decision: Evaluated a fresh 3rd disjoint batch (oos2, 1489 equity funds): frozen official model AUC=0.701 (generalizes), but a naive 3-way retrain scored 0.680 vs the official 0.725 - diagnosed as a composition effect and deliberately NOT promoted; motivated the step7_unified_universe redesign instead.
rationale: Per-origin test AUC (0.708 original / 0.700 oos / 0.668 oos2, with oos2 66% of test rows) plus the frozen model scoring 0.691 on the identical oos2 slice showed the blended drop reflects a harder new population, not corrupted training. Root cause traced to fixed n_clusters=15: oos2's median cluster is 62 funds vs 12 originally, so the cluster-median label and the model's top feature both degrade with population size - a task-definition problem no amount of retraining fixes, hence the redesign (merged universe, constant-size kNN-peer benchmark).

decision: Executed the step7 unified rebuild end-to-end on all 2,243 equity funds (2,087 strategy / 156 allocation, 12 quarters): k=30 strategy clustering (purity 0.504-0.544, ARI 0.229-0.297, zero TDF-dominated clusters), a constant-size kNN-peer-median underperform label (0% of fund-quarters dropped for insufficient peers), and an RF scoring pooled test AUC=0.717 [CI 0.703, 0.729] vs random 0.500. UAT: 8 PASS, 1 MARGINAL (2022q1 alone has 9 clusters under 20 members vs the soft ~6 bar; the other 11 quarters pass).
rationale: The headline AUC needs an honesty caveat that leads, not trails: the persistence baseline scored 0.285, far BELOW 0.5 - peer-relative returns MEAN-REVERT, so raw persistence is an inverted rule and the model's huge 0.43 edge over it (CI [0.407, 0.457], p(edge<=0)=0.0000) is mostly persistence being upside-down. The honest yardstick is reversed persistence (~0.715 = 1 - 0.285); against THAT best naive rule the model's edge is ~0.002, essentially a tie, and the RF - whose top feature is return_vs_peer_median_q - has largely learned that one-line mean-reversion rule. Two framing facts: the peer-perturbation study shows an ~8% label-noise floor (mean flip rate 0.082, 22.2% of fund-quarters flip on >10% of draws, n=22,946) that caps achievable AUC; and the approach-B sanity check (old cluster-median label re-run at k=30) scored 0.689, below the unified model's CI, so the label/feature redefinition - not finer clustering alone - does most of the work. Round-trip verified (reloaded joblib reproduced 20,838 predictions, label metadata intact). Operational lesson from the run: the first attempt died silently when an external process polled the single-writer DuckDB mid-write (a monitoring mistake, not a code bug); the clean re-run succeeded - never open the DuckDB while a pipeline is writing to it.

decision: Received an external review of the published repo (12 claims) and verified it claim-by-claim against local source before adopting anything, rather than implementing the suggestions wholesale or dismissing them. Rejected five claims as wrong or stale with file:line evidence; adopted the landing-page, survivorship-bias, category-look-ahead, and fund-disjoint-split critiques; and converted the review's strongest suggestion (expense-ratio baseline) from 'impossible' to a real work item (step9) after the user's challenge surfaced a structured SEC dataset that makes it honestly buildable.
rationale: Verification split the review cleanly. WRONG or STALE, with evidence: 'add fund size' and 'add prior-quarter relative return' are already features in both model families (predict.py, panel.py BASE_FEATURE_COLS, net_assets_qoq in features.py); no scaler/imputer is fit anywhere (trees + drop-not-impute + stateless get_dummies); clusters are re-fit fresh per quarter and no cluster_id is a model feature; per-quarter AUC/CI/significance, narration caching, a label-noise floor, and a stronger-than-0.5 success bar were all already designed and committed before the review arrived; and 'decisions.json/reflection.html are ceremony without findings' is false for 2 of 3 files - they carry the quantified findings, they just are not surfaced in the README. RIGHT and adopted: the README is a two-line stub, origin/main is 16 commits behind, survivorship bias (fresh ticker map + all-quarters-present filter = survivor-only panel) and category look-ahead (today's yahoo_category stamped onto 2022-2024, LOW severity - enters the model only as ~3.6%-importance tier dummies) are real and now get documented; a fund-disjoint split check is cheap and newly important given the mean-reversion tie. The expense-ratio arc is the lesson: first declined as impossible (N-PORT carries no fee fields; yfinance exposes only today's value = look-ahead), but the user asked what lifting the no-scraping rule would cost, and re-checking surfaced the SEC DERA Mutual Fund Prospectus Risk/Return Summary Data Sets - quarterly bulk XBRL extracts of prospectus fee tables (net/gross expense ratios AND portfolio turnover), as-filed and dated, so point-in-time by construction and fully in-scope under the project's free-bulk-data rules. That makes fees+turnover the most promising route past the mean-reversion tie, and it becomes step9. Meta-lesson recorded deliberately: the external reviewer pattern-matched without reading (5 of 12 claims dissolved on contact with the code), but the two claims that survived verification were exactly the ones nobody inside the project had questioned - external review earns its keep on the blind spots, not the pattern matches.

decision: Built step8 dashboard build.py (the deterministic conductor for the single-file HTML), ran the real end-to-end build against the live step7 DuckDB, generated all 30 per-cluster phi-4 narratives once into a new dashboard_narratives cache table, and passed full browser UAT before committing the built reports/cluster_dashboard.html.
rationale: UAT (Playwright, served offline) passed every design.md item: all 30 cluster sections render with real narratives, member tables, and the lag-probability column carrying the disclaimer dagger; the cluster index lists 30 rows with colour swatches; the scatter map draws 2,086 dots with a working name/ticker/cluster hover tooltip; fund search matches on name and ticker; column sort works ascending and descending with nulls-last; the allocation segment is grouped into 9 vintages (Target-Date + Allocation-by-equity), model-free; the fixed disclaimer footer is always visible; and the scorecard states AUC 0.717, 95% CI 0.703-0.729, the random 0.50 coin-flip baseline, the persistence baseline 0.285 with its honest mean-reversion reading and reversed-rule bar of 0.715, the significance sentence (P(edge<=0)=0.0%), the per-quarter AUC range 0.632-0.763 across 3 quarters, and the 8.2% label-flip stability sentence. Narrative anti-hallucination was spot-checked on clusters 0/14/29: every number, holding, and fund named traces to that cluster grounding payload (only benign LLM rounding, e.g. 0.45%->0.5%). Determinism verified: the full build, a from-cache rebuild after a --skip-narratives run, and a third rebuild all hash identically (sha256 4f83b11f...), because narratives come from the cache table and everything else is a pure function of the step7 tables. Only one network request (the HTML itself) confirms the file is fully offline.

decision: step10 full-universe run complete, and the project's one genuine out-of-time test is in: the dashboard's 2,086 committed-before-the-fact 2024q4->2025q1 predictions scored AUC=0.574 against reality (2,057 scored + 29 died/merged/late + 0 insufficient-peers; base rate 0.532) - real signal, clearly above the 0.500 coin flip, but far below the 0.717 backtest. The frozen step7 model rolled forward over 2025 scored pooled 0.603 (0.636/0.732/0.593/0.429 by quarter, dipping below chance on 2025q4->2026q1); the model retrained on the full 3,035-strategy-fund universe (train 2022-2024q3, test 2024q4-2025q4) scored 0.614 [CI 0.605, 0.623] vs reversed persistence 0.604 - the same 'model ~= the mean-reversion rule plus a sliver' finding as step7, now at a much lower level. Fund-disjoint 80/20 check: 0.603 (no fund-identity memorization). Label-noise floor unchanged at ~8.3% (22.1% of 40,808 fund-quarters flip >10% of peer draws). Dead funds verifiably in the panel: 1,870 labeled rows from 171 dead strategy funds (611 dead series window-wide).
rationale: The honest headline leads: the backtest did NOT survive contact with reality at its advertised level, and the numbers say why rather than hand-waving. The mean-reversion regime the model learned (reversed persistence ~0.715 in the 2024 backtest window) weakened sharply in 2025 (reversed persistence 0.604 on the new test; on the very 2024q4 transition the published predictions cover it was ~0.582, and it inverted outright by 2025q4, where raw persistence 0.551 beat the model's 0.453) - so the model, which had largely learned that one rule, degraded with it. Validation deliberately ran FIRST in build.py, locking the frozen-model numbers into oot_validation before the retrained model existed (no peeking incentive). Attrition was counted, never imputed. Also repaired reproducibly in the same run: funds_full.segment previously existed only via an ad-hoc DB mutation; build.ensure_funds_full_segment now creates it idempotently (regression-tested on a synthetic segment-less table). Read forward, 0.574-with-honest-buckets is worth more than the 0.717 ever was: it is the number a user of the published dashboard actually experienced.

decision: step9 complete: point-in-time expense ratios + portfolio turnover from the SEC DERA Risk/Return Summary datasets were added to the full-universe model, and they help - but only a little. Coverage was near-total (expense 99.2% / turnover 96.9% of the 41,013 labeled rows; 3,031/3,035 strategy funds), so the >=80% gate took the single-model common-covered-subset branch (39,477 rows). On the 2024q4-2025q4 test window: with-fees model AUC 0.619 [0.610, 0.629] vs no-fees 0.615 [0.605, 0.624] on the same subset - a paired fund-clustered edge of [+0.003, +0.006], p=0.000: statistically real, economically tiny. The edge over the reversed-persistence rule (0.604) widens to [+0.010, +0.021], p=0.000 - the model now clears the mean-reversion tie beyond noise. The reviewer's expense-rank baseline scored 0.527 (fees genuinely predict underperformance; the model dominates it by [+0.079, +0.105]). Fund-disjoint 80/20 check: no-fees 0.611 / with-fees 0.614 - no fund fingerprinting. Feature ranks: expense_ratio_net 7th of 17 (0.043), portfolio_turnover 13th (0.020). The 2025q4->2026q1 below-chance quarter (0.468) persists - fees do not fix the regime dependence. Model bundle deliberately NOT saved pending the gate decision.
rationale: Executed on the _full universe (the plan predates step10, so the bars were re-based from 0.715/0.717 to 0.604/0.614). Point-in-time by construction: each (series, quarter) takes the most recent filing dated <= quarter end (0 future-dated sources; assert enforced), fees collapse class->series by MEAN across classes to match how step1 builds the series return the label is defined on. The mandatory net-else-gross fallback (2,890/7,034 sampled keys were gross-only) is recorded per-row in a fee_source column; sanity bounds (expenses [0, 0.10], turnover [0, 20]) null the verified filer garbage (gross max 6.30, turnover max 44,577) without dropping real funds (verified legit net max 0.0598). The honest headline is deliberate: 'fees are a genuine, statistically solid, small improvement' is the publishable finding - not a breakthrough claim the 2025q4 quarter would falsify.

decision: step9 gate decided and step12 dashboard rebuilt on the full 3,231-fund universe. The with-fees model is now the recorded official full-universe model: refit deterministically (seed 42) on the common covered subset (39,477 rows, 17 features) via step9's own fit path and saved as models/full_fees_rf_model.joblib (bundle = model + feature_cols + label_definition), reproducing the chronological test AUC 0.619 [0.610, 0.629] exactly. The fees lift stands as step9 reported it - real but small: +fees clears the reversed-persistence tie (0.604) by [+0.010, +0.021] p=0 and beats no-fees by [+0.003, +0.006] p=0, over the reviewer's expense-rank baseline (0.527). The dashboard was rebuilt against the _full tables (40 peer groups at 2026q1, 2,856 forward predictions) with a redesigned template that LEADS with the out-of-time reality check: published-forward AUC 0.574 (2,057 scored) shown first, the full-universe backtest 0.614 labelled 'for contrast', and the frozen-rolled-forward 0.603 with a per-quarter strip flagging 2025q4 (0.429) as the one quarter of four below the 0.50 coin flip. All 40 cluster narratives regenerated via local phi-4 for 2026q1; the build is byte-deterministic from cache (two rebuilds identical).
rationale: The old _all dashboard advertised a stale 0.717 headline AUC (the unified-universe backtest) with no out-of-time context - the single most misleading number in the project. The rebuild removes it from the headline: the honest 0.574-live-vs-0.614-backtest gap now leads, so a reader meets reality before the backtest. Recording the with-fees bundle closes step9's deferred gate ('deliberately NOT saved pending the gate decision') - the fees improvement is real and reproducible, so the with-fees model is the one to keep. On the reported 'mojibake' in the old clusters 3/10 narrations: investigated to root cause and found none. The stored text, the live phi-4 response, and the rendered HTML all carry a proper U+2014 em-dash; render.py's json.dumps (ensure_ascii=True) escapes it to — and the template ships <meta charset=utf-8>, so it round-trips correctly. The 'mojibake' was the Windows cp1252 console drawing U+2014 as a box - a display artifact, not data corruption - so no code change or lossy cleanup was applied.
